title: Rustic Parsons Farmhouse Table
summary: A chunky farmhouse dining table with solid 4x4 legs and a thick 2x4 plank top — heavy, rustic, and built entirely with pocket screws and self-tapping fasteners.
description:
This is the quintessential rustic farmhouse table: solid 4x4 legs and a top built from eight 2x4s laid flat, giving that thick, substantial plank surface. It seats a crowd and takes abuse, and there's a matching bench that builds the same way. Despite the mass, it's approachable — pocket holes for the top, self-tapping screws for the base.

The top is the star and the trickiest part to get flat. Build it first: pocket-hole all eight 2x4 top boards together with 1-1/2" pocket holes every 8–12" and 2-1/2" screws, minimizing the gaps between boards as you go. Then frame it with the 2x4 side boards (screwed into the ends and the joists) and the 2x3 end joists. Predrill everything — 2x lumber near an end splits easily without it — and keep all fasteners hidden so no holes show on the outside of the finished table.

The legs bolt to the 2x2 under-table joists, and the whole base is self-tapping-screwed together. Rustic doesn't mean crude: sand to at least 120 grit and vacuum clean. And because it's a dining surface that'll meet spills and elbows, you must stain or seal it — bare softwood soaks up every drink and stains permanently. A good exterior-grade or hardwax finish keeps it usable for years.

tools.note: Predrill everything — 2x lumber splits near the ends.

materials.name: 2x3 board, 6 ft
materials.note: End joists (2x2 works if you can't find 2x3).

materials.name: Stain or sealer and topcoat
materials.note: Non-negotiable on a bare-softwood dining surface.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four legs at 29-7/8" from the 4x4. Cut six under-table joists at 30" and two side aprons at 58" from the 2x2. Cut eight tabletop boards at 65" and two tabletop side boards at 58" from the 2x4. Cut two end joists at 28" from the 2x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 10 ft 4x4, four 8 ft 2x2, ten 8 ft 2x4 and one 6 ft 2x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the plank top
steps.body: Build the tabletop first: pocket-hole all eight 2x4 top boards together with 1-1/2" pocket holes every 8–12" and 2-1/2" screws, keeping the gaps between boards minimal. A flat, tight top is the whole look.

steps.title: Add the top ends
steps.body: Add the end boards, minimizing the gap to the top boards, predrilling and screwing down with 2-1/2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Add the side boards
steps.body: Add the 2x4 side aprons/boards, screwing into both the tabletop end boards and the joists with glue and 2-1/2" screws. Predrill all holes so nothing splits.

steps.title: Attach the joists and legs
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 under-table joists to the underside, then bolt the four 4x4 legs to the joists with 3" self-tapping screws and glue. Check the base is level and square.

steps.title: Sand and seal
steps.body: There should be no exposed holes on the outside of the table. Sand with 120 grit and vacuum clean. Then stain or seal and topcoat — a bare softwood dining top soaks up every spill and stains permanently, so this step is not optional.

images.alt: Rustic farmhouse dining table with 4x4 legs and a thick plank top
